title: Somatic Experiencing for Sensory Overload

body:
Somatic Experiencing for Sensory Overload

You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 AM, staring at the ceiling, or maybe you're curled up somewhere, trying to make sense of the buzzing, the lights, the sheer too muchness of everything. You're not alone. I've been there. I know that feeling of your nerves being frayed, like every sound is a scream, every light a spotlight directly on your raw, exposed brain. It's not just "stress," is it? It's something else, something deeper, something that feels like it's ripping you apart from the inside out. That's sensory overload, and if you're dealing with a spiritual emergency - like a kundalini awakening gone sideways - it's a constant companion.

I'll tell you what - when I was in the thick of my own crisis, I thought I was losing my mind. The world felt like it was dialed up to eleven, and I couldn't find the volume knob. Every conversation was a cacophony, every texture on my skin felt like sandpaper, every smell was overwhelming. My nervous system was just fried. It's like your body is screaming, "TOO MUCH! CAN'T HANDLE THIS!" and you're just stuck in the middle of it. You're probably looking for answers, for something, anything, to make it stop. I get it. I really do.

What I found, what actually helped me claw my way back, wasn't more meditation, wasn't more spiritual practices. Honestly, those often made it worse, at least initially. What helped was something called Somatic Experiencing (SE). It's not about "fixing" you, because you're not broken. It's about helping your body remember how to regulate itself, how to come back down from that constant state of alarm. Because that's what sensory overload is, you know? It's your nervous system stuck in fight, flight, or freeze. It's trying to protect you, but it's doing it in a way that feels like torture.

So yeah, let's talk about SE. It's a body-oriented approach to healing trauma. And trust me, what you're going through - whether it's a full-blown kundalini crisis or something else that's left you feeling shattered - it's traumatic. Your body is holding onto all that energy, all that fear, all that overwhelm. SE helps gently release it, without re-traumatizing you. That's the key. You don't need to relive it; you need to discharge it. Right?

Understanding the Overwhelmed Nervous System

Look, your nervous system has two main modes: sympathetic and parasympathetic. Sympathetic is your gas pedal - fight, flight, freeze. Parasympathetic is your brake - rest and digest. When you're experiencing sensory overload, you're stuck on the gas pedal. Your body thinks there's a tiger chasing you, even if you're just sitting in your living room. The lights are too bright because your pupils are dilated, ready to spot danger. Sounds are too loud because your ears are hyper-vigilant. Your skin feels raw because every sensation is being filtered through an alarm system.

This isn't a mental problem. It's a biological one. It's your ancient survival mechanisms gone haywire. And when you're in a spiritual emergency, like a kundalini awakening, it's often because a massive amount of energy has been unleashed, and your system isn't prepared to handle it. It's like plugging a high-voltage appliance into a low-voltage outlet. Blown fuse, right? Your nervous system is that blown fuse.

I remember trying to explain this to friends, and they'd just look at me blankly. "Just relax," they'd say. "Take a deep breath." Oh, if only it were that simple. When your system is in full-blown panic, telling yourself to relax is like telling a drowning person to just swim harder. It doesn't work. In fact, it often makes you feel worse, more inadequate, more alone. You know what I mean? You're not failing at relaxing; your body literally can't relax in that state.

The goal with SE isn't to force relaxation. It's to help your body naturally find its way back to balance. It's about titration - releasing small bits of stored energy at a time, so you don't get overwhelmed again. And pendulation - moving between states of activation and states of relative calm. It's slow work, sometimes painfully slow, but it's effective. It's like slowly letting the air out of a balloon, rather than popping it. Does that land for you?

How Somatic Experiencing Helps with Sensory Overload

So, how does SE actually work for this hellish sensory overload? It's all about tracking sensation. Instead of focusing on the story of what happened, or trying to rationalize why you feel this way, an SE practitioner will guide you to notice what's happening in your body, right now. "Where do you feel that buzzing?" "What's the texture of that anxiety?" "Is there a color to that overwhelm?"

Sounds weird, I know. When I first heard it, I was like, "Are you kidding me? I'm already overwhelmed by sensations!" But here's the thing: you're not trying to push the sensations away. You're learning to be with them, in tiny, manageable doses, and allowing your body to complete the stress response cycle that got interrupted. Because that's what trauma is - an incomplete stress response. Your body got stuck in fight/flight/freeze, and it never got to discharge that energy. So it's just sitting there, humming away, making everything feel too much.

An SE practitioner will help you find resources - things that bring you a sense of calm or safety, even if it's just a tiny flicker. Maybe it's the feeling of your feet on the ground, or the warmth of your hands, or a memory of a peaceful place. Then, they'll gently guide you to notice a sensation that's a little bit activating - maybe a tiny bit of that buzzing, or a slight tension. And then, you'll go back to your resource. It's like dipping your toe in the cold water, then pulling it back out, over and over, until your body gets used to the temperature. This gradual exposure, this titration, is what helps your nervous system slowly expand its capacity to handle sensation without going into full alarm.

I remember one session where I was just gripped by this intense internal vibration, like my whole body was humming with electricity. My SE practitioner didn't tell me to ignore it. She asked me to notice where it was strongest, what temperature it was, if it had a texture. And then she asked me to notice the feeling of the chair beneath me, the solidity of the ground. We went back and forth, from the vibration to the ground, from the ground to the vibration, in tiny sips. And slowly, almost imperceptibly, the vibration started to soften, to spread out, to become less intense. It wasn't gone, but it wasn't overwhelming anymore. It was just a sensation. That's the magic. It teaches your body that these sensations aren't going to kill you, that you can actually tolerate them.

Practical Steps and Resources

Okay, so you're probably thinking, "This sounds good, but what do I actually do?" Finding a certified Somatic Experiencing practitioner is your best bet. You can look on the Somatic Experiencing International website for a directory. This isn't something you want to try to figure out entirely on your own, especially when you're feeling so vulnerable. A good practitioner will create a safe container for you to do this work.

In the meantime, there are some things you can start practicing on your own, very gently. These aren't substitutes for working with a practitioner, but they can help you start to build some capacity:

  • Orienting: This is super simple, but powerful. Just slowly look around your room, really noticing objects. Don't label them, just let your eyes take them in. Notice colors, shapes, textures. Let your gaze linger on something that feels neutral or pleasant. This helps your brain realize you're actually safe in your present environment, not in some past danger.
  • Grounding: Feel your feet on the floor. Really feel them. Notice the contact, the pressure, the temperature. Or feel your butt in the chair. This brings your awareness back into your body and into the present moment, anchoring you.
  • Tracking Sensation (Gently): If you notice an uncomfortable sensation, like a buzzing or tightness, try to just notice it without judgment. Where is it? What's its size? What's its temperature? And then, immediately, shift your attention to a part of your body that feels neutral or even pleasant. Maybe your right pinky finger feels fine. Or your earlobe. Just go back and forth. Small doses.
  • Pendulation Practice: Think of something that feels mildly activating - not terrifying, just a little bit. Hold it in your awareness for a few seconds. Then, consciously shift your attention to something that feels calming or resourcing. Maybe a cherished memory, or the feeling of a soft blanket. Go back and forth. You're teaching your nervous system that it can move between states.

I remember trying to read when I was in the thick of it, and it was impossible. But slowly, as I started to do these practices, I could actually pick up a book again. And some books really helped me understand what the hell was happening. If you're looking for some further reading, I'd suggest these. They're not SE specific, but they talk about the bigger picture of spiritual emergency and kundalini, which can help you feel less crazy:

  • For understanding the broader context of spiritual emergencies: The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link)
  • If you suspect it's kundalini, this one's a classic: The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link)
  • And two more that were incredibly helpful for me in seeing I wasn't alone: When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link). Seriously, reading those felt like someone was finally speaking my language.

The Long Road Back

This isn't a quick fix, you know. There's no magic pill that just turns off the sensory overload. It's a process. It's about patiently, gently, teaching your nervous system to trust again, to feel safe again. And that takes time. I'm not going to lie to you - there will be days where you feel like you're going backward. Days where the noise is too much, the lights are too bright, and you just want to curl up in a ball and disappear. That's normal. That's part of the process. Don't beat yourself up for it.

But here's the thing: you're not stuck. You're not doomed to live like this forever. Your body wants to heal. It has an innate capacity for self-regulation. We just need to help it remember how. Somatic Experiencing doesn't add anything new to you; it helps remove the blocks that are preventing your natural healing mechanisms from doing their job.

I remember feeling so isolated. Like no one could possibly understand what I was going through. And maybe no one in your immediate circle does. But there are people out there - practitioners, other survivors - who get it. You're not alone in this. And the fact that you're even looking for answers, that you're still fighting, that's a huge sign of your resilience. Don't forget that.

Be kind to yourself. Rest when you need to rest. Say no to things that feel like too much. Protect your energy fiercely. Your primary job right now is to create as much safety and calm for your nervous system as possible. That might mean dimming the lights, wearing earplugs, spending time in nature, or just sitting in silence. Listen to your body. It's trying to tell you what it needs, even if it's screaming it at you right now.

Integration and Beyond

Once you start to get a handle on the sensory overload, once your nervous system starts to settle, then you can begin the deeper work of integration. What was that spiritual emergency trying to tell you? What changes does it demand in your life? Because these experiences, as terrifying as they are, often come with a deep message, a call for radical transformation.

But you can't hear that message when you're in constant fight-or-flight. You can't integrate anything when your system is screaming. First, you need to feel safe in your own skin again. You need to reclaim your body as a place of refuge, not a torture chamber. That's what Somatic Experiencing helps you do.

I'm not going to tell you it's easy. It's not. It's probably the hardest thing you'll ever do. But it's worth it. To feel the world again, not as an assault, but as it is. To hear music without flinching. To see colors without pain. To just exist in your body without that constant hum of alarm. That's freedom. And it's possible for you. I know it is, because I've walked that path. You've got this, even if it doesn't feel like it right now. Just take one small step at a time. One breath. One moment of feeling your feet on the ground. You'll get there.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
